target.name: Spirotetracyclic Core of the Ginkgolides (9)
target.motivation: The ginkgolides are a family of terpene trilactone natural products with significant biological activity. Their structural complexity, including a tert-butyl group, six heavily functionalized five-membered rings, and up to 12 stereogenic centers (two of which are contiguous all-carbon quaternary centers), makes their synthesis challenging. This work aims to develop a concise synthesis of the spirotetracyclic core, specifically addressing the construction of the challenging all-carbon quaternary centers.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: [2+2] ketene cycloaddition and subsequent ring expansion to construct adjacent quaternary centers
[2+2] cycloaddition to forge contiguous quaternary stereocenters
diastereoselective Claisen rearrangement followed by an enolate alkylation for adjacent all-carbon stereocenters
[3+2] palladium-catalyzed cycloaddition to synthesize spirocycle 6 (truncated analogue)
alkylation of cyclopentadiene and oxidation with singlet oxygen (truncated analogue)
Pd(II)-catalyzed oxidative cyclization to synthesize spirotricycle 8 (truncated analogue)

success_criteria.free_text: Diastereoselective synthesis of rings A, B, D and E of the natural product, featuring an oxidative radical cascade to forge the key adjacent all-carbon quaternary centers.

key_disconnections.reasoning: Ring E would be built last via olefin cleavage, α-hydroxylation and lactonization of the newly installed hydroxy group with one of the diastereotopic methyl esters of the malonate of tricycle 22.
The oxidative radical cascade of γ-butenolide 23 was envisaged to construct rings A, B and D of the ginkgolide core (22) and set the relative configuration of two stereocenters.

key_disconnections.bond_description: γ-butenolide 23 from aldehyde 24 and vinyl iodide 25
key_disconnections.reasoning: γ-butenolide 23 would be prepared through the addition of the organomagnesium reagent derived from alkenyl iodide 25 to aldehyde 24, employing chemistry developed by Knochel and co-workers.
Aldehyde 24 was obtained in three steps from pivalaldehyde (27) through an indium-mediated Knoevenagel condensation, Sakurai allylation and Lemieux-Johnson oxidation.

Vinyl iodide 25 was prepared in two steps from propargyl chloride (29) via alkynyl ester 33, obtained through one-pot substitution with allylmagnesium bromide and methoxycarbonylation, followed by conjugate addition of iodide.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The proposed route leverages a manganese(III)-mediated oxidative radical cascade for the rapid and diastereoselective construction of the challenging all-carbon quaternary centers, a departure from previous pericyclic strategies. The tert-butyl group is incorporated early from pivalaldehyde, avoiding the use of tert-butyl lithium-derived organocopper reagents.

thought.background: The retrosynthetic analysis identified aldehyde 24 as a key intermediate, derivable from simple starting materials.
thought.gap: Need to synthesize aldehyde 24 efficiently and stereoselectively.
thought.decision: To synthesize aldehyde 24, chose a three-step sequence starting from pivalaldehyde (27) involving indium-mediated Knoevenagel condensation, Sakurai allylation, and Lemieux-Johnson oxidation, expecting a high-yielding and selective route.

outcome.observation: Aldehyde 24 was obtained in three steps from pivalaldehyde (27) through an indium-mediated Knoevenagel condensation, Sakurai allylation and Lemieux-Johnson oxidation.

evidence.text_span: Aldehyde 24 was obtained in three steps from pivalaldehyde (27) through an indiummediated Knoevenagel condensation,41 Sakurai allylation and Lemieux-Johnson oxidation.42
evidence.section: Synthesis of Aldehyde 24 and Vinyl Iodide 25

thought.background: The retrosynthetic analysis identified vinyl iodide 25 as another key intermediate, derivable from simple starting materials.
thought.gap: Need to synthesize vinyl iodide 25 efficiently and stereoselectively.
thought.decision: To synthesize vinyl iodide 25, chose a two-step sequence starting from propargyl chloride (29) involving one-pot substitution with allylmagnesium bromide and methoxycarbonylation, followed by conjugate addition of iodide, expecting a stereoselective formation of the (Z)-vinyl iodide.

outcome.observation: Vinyl iodide 25 was prepared in two steps from propargyl chloride (29). First, alkynyl ester 33 was obtained through the one-pot substitution of propargyl chloride (29) with allylmagnesium bromide and methoxycarbonylation of the resulting alkynylmagnesium as developed by Hopf. Subsequently, conjugate addition of iodide under acidic conditions provided vinyl iodide 25 from alkynyl ester 33. The synthesis of vinyl iodide 25 was highly stereoselective, yielding exclusively the (Z)-vinyl iodide required.

evidence.text_span: Vinyl iodide 25 was prepared in two steps from propargyl chloride (29). First, alkynyl ester 33 was obtained through the one-pot substitution of propargyl chloride (29) with allylmagnesium bromide and methoxycarbonylation of the resulting alkynylmagnesium as developed by Hopf.43 Subsequently, conjugate addition of iodide under acidic conditions provided vinyl iodide 25 from alkynyl ester 33. 44
evidence.section: Synthesis of Aldehyde 24 and Vinyl Iodide 25

thought.background: Aldehyde 24 and vinyl iodide 25 are now available.
thought.gap: Need to synthesize γ-butenolide 23, a key precursor for the radical cascade, from 24 and 25.
thought.decision: To synthesize γ-butenolide 23, chose to treat aldehyde 24 with the Grignard reagent derived from vinyl iodide 25 in the presence of trimethylsilyl chloride, expecting the formation of γ-butenolide 23.

outcome.observation: Treatment of aldehyde 24 with the Grignard reagent derived from vinyl iodide 25 in the presence of trimethylsilyl chloride yielded γ-butenolide 23' as the major diastereomer (4:1 d.r.) in 85% yield, having the undesired syn-relative configuration. Diastereoisomers 23 and 23' were inseparable and their relative configuration could not be determined from NMR analysis. Instead, their relative configuration was assigned from the configurations of the oxidative cyclization products (vide infra).

evidence.text_span: Treatment of aldehyde 24 with the Grignard reagent derived from vinyl iodide 25 in the presence of trimethylsilyl chloride yielded γ-butenolide 23′ as the major diastereomer (4:1 d.r.) in 85% yield, having the undesired synrelative configuration. Diastereoisomers 23 and 23′ were inseparable and their relative configuration could not be determined from NMR analysis. Instead, their relative configuration was assigned from the configurations of the oxidative cyclization products (vide infra).
evidence.section: Synthesis of Aldehyde 24 and Vinyl Iodide 25

thought.background: The Grignard addition yielded γ-butenolide 23' as the major diastereomer (4:1 d.r.) with the undesired syn-relative configuration.
thought.gap: Need to obtain the desired anti-isomer 23.
thought.decision: Attempted to epimerize γ-butenolide 23' to mixtures of 23' and 23 under acidic or basic conditions, expecting to achieve the desired diastereomeric ratio.

outcome.observation: Attempts to epimerize γ-butenolide 23' to mixtures of 23' and 23 under acidic or basic conditions were unsuccessful and led to recovery of γ-butenolide 23'.

evidence.text_span: Attempts to epimerize γ-butenolide 23′ to mixtures of 23′ and 23 under acidic or basic conditions were unsuccessful and led to recovery of γ-butenolide 23′ (see Supporting Information, S53).
evidence.section: Synthesis of Aldehyde 24 and Vinyl Iodide 25

thought.background: Direct epimerization of γ-butenolide 23' was unsuccessful.
thought.gap: Still need to obtain the desired anti-isomer 23.
thought.decision: To obtain the desired anti-isomer 23, chose to isomerize γ-butenolide 23' through silylation to give the corresponding silyloxyfuran, followed by desilylation in acetic acid, expecting to yield the desired anti-isomer 23 in a favorable diastereomeric ratio.

outcome.observation: The isomerization of γ-butenolide 23' into 23 was ultimately achieved through silylation of γ-butenolide 23' to give the corresponding silyloxyfuran, followed by desilylation in acetic acid. This protocol yielded the desired anti-isomer 23 in a 3:1 diastereomeric ratio with 23'. The desilylation reaction was highly sensitive to the acid employed, with other acids yielding mixtures favoring the syn-isomer 23' or containing the undesired α-butenolide.

evidence.text_span: The isomerization of γ-butenolide 23′ into 23 was ultimately achieved through silylation of γ-butenolide 23′ to give the corresponding silyloxyfuran, followed by desilylation in acetic acid. This protocol yielded the desired anti-isomer 23 in a 3:1 diastereomeric ratio with 23′. The desilylation reaction was highly sensitive to the acid employed, with other acids yielding mixtures favoring the syn-isomer 23′ or containing the undesired α-butenolide (see Supporting Information, S53).
evidence.section: Synthesis of Aldehyde 24 and Vinyl Iodide 25

thought.background: The desired anti-isomer 23 (containing 25% of syn-diastereoisomer 23') is now available.
thought.gap: Need to perform the key oxidative radical cascade to form tricycle 22.
thought.decision: To form tricycle 22, chose to treat γ-butenolide 23 with Mn(OAc)3 and Cu(OAc)2 in DMSO, expecting a diastereoselective cyclization.

outcome.observation: The oxidative radical cascade of γ-butenolide 23 (containing 25% of syn-diastereoisomer 23') yielded tricycle 22 in 50% yield and excellent diastereoselectivity. The diasterocontrol likely arises from the kinetic and thermodynamic preference to form cis-fused bicyclo[3.3.0]octanes coupled with the tert-butyl group residing on the less hindered convex face of the forming bicyclooctane.

evidence.text_span: The oxidative radical cascade of γ-butenolide 23 (containing 25% of syn-diastereoisomer 23′) yielded tricycle 22 in 50% yield and excellent diastereoselectivity (Scheme 1). The diasterocontrol likely arises from the kinetic and thermodynamic preference to form cis-fused bicyclo[3.3.0]octanes coupled with the tert-butyl group residing on the less hindered convex face of the forming bicyclooctane.46,47
evidence.section: Synthesis of Aldehyde 24 and Vinyl Iodide 25

thought.gap: Need to confirm the relative configuration of tricycle 22.
thought.decision: To confirm the relative configuration of tricycle 22, chose to perform Lemieux-Johnson oxidation to obtain cyclopentanone 35, followed by X-ray crystallography.

outcome.observation: The relative configuration of tricycle 22 was subsequently confirmed by X-ray crystallography of cyclopentanone 35, obtained via Lemieux-Johnson oxidation of 22.

evidence.text_span: The relative configuration of tricycle 22 was subsequently confirmed by Xray crystallography of cyclopentanone 35, obtained via Lemieux-Johnson oxidation of 22. (Scheme 1).
evidence.section: Synthesis of Aldehyde 24 and Vinyl Iodide 25

thought.background: Cyclopentanone 35 is available. The last step is to construct ring E via hydroxylation and lactonization.
thought.gap: Need to hydroxylate cyclopentanone 35 at the α-position and then lactonize.
thought.decision: Based on precedent in total syntheses of ginkgolide B, initially envisaged deprotonation of cyclopentanone 35 with lithium diethylamide followed by oxidation of the so-formed lithium enolate with Davis oxaziridine, expecting to yield the required product 9.

outcome.observation: Unfortunately, under these reaction conditions cyclopentanone 35 degraded and no hydroxylation products were observed.

evidence.text_span: Based on precedent in the total syntheses of ginkgolide B by Corey and Crimmins, we initially envisaged deprotonation of cyclopentanone 35 with lithium diethylamide followed by oxidation of the so-formed lithium enolate with Davis oxaziridine could yield the required product 9 (Scheme 2).15,16,18,1 9 Unfortunately, under these reaction conditions cyclopentanone 35 degraded and no hydroxylation products were observed.

thought.background: The initial attempt at α-hydroxylation of cyclopentanone 35 failed due to degradation.
thought.gap: Need an alternative method for α-hydroxylation.
thought.decision: Attempted alternative oxidants, specifically tetrabutylammonium fluoride/dimethyldioxirane (TBAF/DMDO), expecting to achieve hydroxylation.

outcome.observation: Alternative oxidants were equally unsuccessful in this reaction, with tetrabutylammonium fluoride/dimethyldioxirane (TBAF/DMDO), giving instead the δ-lactone 39, the product of formal Baeyer−Villiger oxidation.

evidence.text_span: Alternative oxidants were equally unsuccessful in this reaction, with tetrabutylammonium fluoride/dimethyldioxirane (TBAF/DMDO), giving instead the δ-lactone 39, the product of formal Baeyer−Villiger oxidation.

thought.background: Previous α-hydroxylation attempts failed. An alternative two-step sequence involving Mn(OAc)3-mediated oxidation in the presence of TEMPO followed by reductive N-O bond cleavage was envisioned.
thought.gap: Need to form alkoxyamine 37 as an intermediate for α-hydroxylation.
thought.decision: To form alkoxyamine 37, chose to treat cyclopentanone 35 with Mn(OAc)3 and TEMPO, expecting the formation of alkoxyamine 37.

outcome.observation: However, upon treatment of cyclopentanone 35 with Mn(OAc)3 and TEMPO, alkoxyamine 37 was not observed and hydroxyenone 36 was obtained instead in 46% yield. We hypothesized enone 36 was formed via Kornblum-DeLaMare-like rearrangement of TEMPO adduct 38, which was preferentially formed over adduct 37 presumably due to the steric hindrance around C(6) in cyclopentanone 35.

evidence.text_span: However, upon treatment of cyclopentanone 35 with Mn(OAc)3 and TEMPO, alkoxyamine 37 was not observed and hydroxyenone 36 was obtained instead in 46% yield. We hypothesized enone 36 was formed via Kornblum-DeLaMare-like rearrangement of TEMPO adduct 38, which was preferentially formed over adduct 37 presumably due to the steric hindrance around C(6) in cyclopentanone 35.

thought.background: The Mn(OAc)3/TEMPO oxidation yielded an undesired hydroxyenone due to steric hindrance.
thought.gap: Need to form the desired C(6)-alkoxyamine 40 for α-hydroxylation.
thought.decision: Based on the hypothesis of steric hindrance, attempted the same α-oxidation reaction of cyclopentanone 35 with the less sterically demanding oxyl radical ABNO, expecting to yield the desired C(6)-alkoxyamine 40.

outcome.observation: Indeed, this reaction gave a completely different outcome, yielding desired C(6)-alkoxyamine 40 in 84% yield.

evidence.text_span: Based on this hypothesis, we attempted this same α-oxidation reaction of cyclopentanone 35 with the less sterically demanding oxyl radical ABNO. Indeed, this reaction gave a completely different outcome, yielding desired C(6)-alkoxyamine 40 in 84% yield.

thought.gap: Need to cleave the N-O bond to obtain the hydroxylated product 41.
thought.decision: To obtain alcohol 41, chose palladium-catalyzed hydrogenolysis of alkoxyamine 40, expecting the required N-O bond cleavage.

outcome.observation: Palladium-catalyzed hydrogenolysis of alkoxyamine 40 resulted in the required N-O bond cleavage to give alcohol 41.

evidence.text_span: Palladium-catalyzed hydrogenolysis of alkoxyamine 40 resulted in the required N-O bond cleavage to give alcohol 41.

thought.background: Alcohol 41 has been obtained. The final step is lactonization to form the spirotetracyclic core 9.
thought.gap: Need to lactonize alcohol 41 to form 9.
thought.decision: To lactonize alcohol 41, chose a mild, silica-mediated lactonization procedure, after observing that silica facilitated the lactonization during chromatographic purification. The formal hydroxylation and lactonization of tricycle 35 could ultimately be performed in a one-pot fashion after minor modifications.

outcome.observation: The lactonization of alcohol 41 to give the core of the ginkgolides (9) was, contrary to our initial predictions, not spontaneous. Small quantities of tetracycle 9, however, were detected upon chromatographic purification of alcohol 41. The observation that silica was facilitating the lactonization of alcohol 41 allowed the development of a mild, silica-mediated lactonization procedure. The formal hydroxylation and lactonization of tricycle 35 could ultimately be performed in a one-pot fashion after minor modifications, yielding the spirocyclic core (9), containing rings A, B, D, and E of the ginkgolides, in 64% isolated yield from 35.

evidence.text_span: The lactonization of alcohol 41 to give the core of the ginkgolides (9) was, contrary to our initial predictions, not spontaneous. Small quantities of tetracycle 9, however, were detected upon chromatographic purification of alcohol 41. The observation that silica was facilitating the lactonization of alcohol 41 allowed the development of a mild, silica-mediated lactonization procedure. The formal hydroxylation and lactonization of tricycle 35 could ultimately be performed in a one-pot fashion after minor modifications, yielding the spirocyclic core (9), containing rings A, B, D, and E of the ginkgolides, in 64% isolated yield from 35.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: Total number of linear steps in the synthesis.

validation.significance: This concise synthesis of the spirotetracyclic core of the ginkgolides (9) in 9 steps and 4.0% overall yield successfully addresses the challenging construction of contiguous all-carbon stereocenters via a highly diastereoselective Mn(OAc)3-mediated oxidative cascade, representing a departure from previous pericyclic strategies. The early incorporation of the tert-butyl group from pivalaldehyde is also a notable improvement. This work lays the foundations for the total synthesis of the ginkgolides.

source.title: Synthesis of the Spirotetracyclic Core of the Ginkgolides via a Malonyl Radical Cascade.
source.authors: Hernández-Lladó P, Christensen KE, Burton JW.
